cold_start_fixture.rationale: Breadth-first opening — one question per foundational pillar to build a plan skeleton before depth-first sweeps.

mom_test_rejection_patterns.replace_with: Tell me about the last time you spent money trying to solve this.
How are you solving this today, and what is that costing you in dollars and hours per month?
Why do you bother dealing with this at all?
Describe the workaround you'd use if you couldn't have any solution at all.
Walk me through the day you tried to solve this and gave up.
Have you actually talked to customers about this? Quote them.

questions.question: Pick exactly one primary revenue model from: subscription, transactional, marketplace take-rate, ads, lead-gen, license, services, freemium-to-paid, usage-based. State it in one sentence and name a public company that runs the same model.
questions.rationale: forces explicit selection — Lean Canvas block 9

questions.question: Sketch your unit-of-value: name the smallest thing a customer buys, what they pay for it, how often they buy.
Will you publish prices on your site at launch, or gate them behind "contact us"? Defend the choice in one sentence.
questions.rationale: transparency vs. enterprise-sales motion is a defining MVP UX decision

questions.question: What is the highest price a serious buyer would genuinely pay before walking away? Anchor against a named competitor's pricing page.
At what price would a buyer wonder if the product is real or undercapitalized?
questions.rationale: van Westendorp lower bound — under-pricing signals risk

questions.question: Three named direct competitors' published price points (with URLs). Where does your price sit relative to each?
questions.rationale: pricing in a vacuum is the most common founder mistake

questions.question: If your pricing is wrong by 2x in either direction, which direction is more lethal in your market — too high or too low? Defend.
Is there a free tier, a free trial, both, or neither? Name the conversion event the funnel optimizes toward.
questions.rationale: funnel design depends entirely on this

questions.question: Annual contracts, monthly, or both? Defend the default.
questions.rationale: cash-flow shape and churn measurement both hinge here

questions.question: If you ship one paid tier vs. three at launch, which do you pick and why?
questions.rationale: tier proliferation pre-PMF is a known anti-pattern

questions.question: What is your gross margin target at scale, and what is the dominant variable cost that determines it?
questions.rationale: margin profile predicts which fundraising path is even available

questions.question: If a customer renews after 12 months, what specifically changed for them that made renewal a no-brainer?
questions.rationale: retention thesis = the truest PMF signal (Jackson / Reforge)

questions.question: Self-serve, inside-sales, field-sales, channel — pick one as the default motion for MVP.
questions.rationale: every other GTM decision flows from this

questions.question: Average deal size, p50 and p90, at MVP and at month 12. Two numbers each.
Which billing provider — Stripe, Lemonsqueezy, Paddle, Chargebee, in-house? Defend.
Refund policy in one sentence.
questions.rationale: legal-team won't write this for you; founder decides

questions.question: Will you take payment in non-USD currencies? Which ones, by when?
Does the business require any one customer to be larger than 10% of revenue at any stage?
questions.rationale: customer-concentration risk is binary — name it now

questions.question: If you wanted to double prices in year 2, what would have to be true?
questions.rationale: pricing power requires planning, not luck

questions.question: If a customer asks for a feature in exchange for an expansion deal, what's your default — build, charge, decline?
questions.rationale: the most common services-creep trap for SaaS

questions.question: Will MVP support discounts (coupon codes, annual prepay, design-partner)? If yes, what's your discount discipline policy?
What's the unit-economics breakeven price — the floor below which the model collapses on volume?
questions.rationale: founders often discover this only after they've quoted it

questions.question: Will you have a Sales-Assisted-Self-Serve (SAS) motion in year 1, or pure PLG, or pure sales-led?
For B2B: net-30, net-60, prepay-only?
How many entities (people, businesses, transactions) exist that *could* buy this? Times what each would spend per year. Show the multiplication and cite the source for the count.
Cite a published market-size report for your category. Name the analyst, the year, the methodology, the URL.
If bottom-up and top-down TAM disagree by > 3x, which one are you choosing and why?
Of TAM, who can your distribution channels and price point actually reach in the first three years? Show the cut.
questions.rationale: SAM is the most-faked number on pitch decks

questions.question: Of SAM, what share is realistic in 18 months given your headcount and budget? Cite the comparable benchmark you're indexing against.
If your SOM exceeds 5% of SAM in year 1, name the comparable that hit similar share at similar stage.
questions.rationale: otherwise you're claiming a category-best result by default

questions.question: What is happening *now* that wasn't true 24 months ago that makes this the moment?
What's happening 24 months from now that, if you wait, kills the window?
Has the underlying technology recently dropped in price or jumped in capability? Name the curve.
Regulatory tailwind or headwind? Name the specific rule, jurisdiction, and effective date.
questions.rationale: regulation either opens or closes markets; vague answers = vague exposure

questions.question: What customer behavior had to shift for this to be possible? When did the shift happen?
If the market is growing, what is the growth rate and the source?
questions.rationale: TAM and growth-rate together set the upper bound of the prize

questions.question: Three geographies ranked: where you launch, where you expand to in year 2, where you ignore entirely.
questions.rationale: founders who try to launch globally usually launch nowhere

questions.question: Is this category invented by you, or are you taking share from incumbents?
questions.rationale: category creation and category capture are different playbooks

questions.question: If you're inventing a category, who else is naming it? Three other companies — or accept that you're alone (with the higher risk that implies).
What adjacent market do you move into in year 3 if MVP works? Don't build for it — just name it.
Name a previous wave of companies that tried this and died. Why did they die, and what's changed?
questions.rationale: if you can't name the graveyard, you don't know the category

questions.question: Is your market concentrated (top 3 incumbents hold > 60%) or fragmented?
How big is the white-space you target — the cohort no incumbent serves well?
Is this market seasonal? If yes, when's the buying season and what's your launch-timing implication?
questions.rationale: ship Q1 if customers buy in Q4

questions.question: Are your market estimates distorted by COVID-era anomalies (lockdown surges, supply-chain shocks)?
Where in the Gartner hype cycle is your category? (Innovation trigger / peak / trough / slope / plateau.)
What would have to be true for the right answer to be "don't build this"? Steelman the case against.
For SEO and content: which country's search index actually matters in year 1 — global, single country, single city?
What distribution channel is freshly open (e.g., TikTok 2018, ChatGPT plugins 2023, agent marketplaces 2026) that an incumbent can't easily exploit?
Which incumbents' adjacent products could swallow yours if they decided to ship it as a feature? Name three.
Name your ICP at the firm level using firmographics: industry, employee count range, revenue range, geography, growth stage.
What stack are they already running? Name three tools you assume they use today.
What trigger event makes them start looking for a solution? Name it as an observable signal a Clay or Apollo lookup could detect.
Buyer and user — same person, or different? If different, who has final sign-off and who feels the daily pain?
questions.rationale: misaligned buyer/user is the #1 enterprise sales failure mode

questions.question: Fill the JTBD sentence in a real customer's own words: *"When I am [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]."*
Name the functional, emotional, and social dimensions of that job. All three.
questions.rationale: if the founder can only name functional, the JTBD is incomplete

questions.question: Tell me about the last time your prospective customer faced this problem. What did they actually *do*? (Not what they say they'd do.)
questions.rationale: Mom Test — chase history, not hypotheticals

questions.question: Have you talked to 10 of these customers yet? 5? 1? Zero? Honest answer — it changes the next 30 questions.
questions.rationale: customer-discovery debt is the most-hidden founder weakness

questions.question: Who is your negative persona — a type of buyer you'd refuse to serve even if they paid? Name the reason.
Channel #1: where do you find the next 100 customers? Be specific — "outbound LinkedIn," "SEO term X," "paid ads on Y," "partnership with Z."
Channel #2 (the backup if #1 underperforms): name it.
questions.rationale: single-channel risk is a known killer

questions.question: CAC ceiling: most you can spend acquiring one customer before unit economics break?
Sales cycle length, p50 and p90 — two numbers in days or weeks.
questions.rationale: runway planning depends on cash-conversion speed

questions.question: Who is the *current* alternative the customer uses — including "doing nothing"?
questions.rationale: "doing nothing" is the most common winner; you must beat it

questions.question: What's the switching cost from the alternative — in dollars, hours, retraining?
questions.rationale: switching cost defines deal-size and time-to-close

questions.question: What persona do you secretly suspect will be the *real* buyer once you launch — different from the one you're targeting?
questions.rationale: founders often pivot to a buyer they ruled out on day 1

questions.question: Persona age band, role title, level of authority, one personality trait.
What does this persona read, watch, listen to weekly? Name three sources.
In a target buyer org, who else needs to approve the purchase? Name the org-chart roles.
questions.rationale: procurement committees kill more deals than competitors

questions.question: Above what deal size does the buyer escalate to a budget owner? Name the threshold.
questions.rationale: the threshold determines self-serve vs. sales-assisted

questions.question: Which professional communities (Slack, Discord, subreddit, LinkedIn group) does this persona inhabit? List three.
questions.rationale: community-led acquisition is cheaper than ads

questions.question: Walk me through a "Tuesday at 2pm" narrative for this persona. What's on their screen? What just interrupted them?
Which conferences or events does this persona attend annually? Name two.
What does the *expansion-account* profile look like — the persona/account that goes from $10K → $100K with you?
When the buyer describes the problem internally, what words do they use? Quote three.
How much money has this persona already spent trying to solve this problem? Tools? Consultants? Internal projects?
questions.rationale: existing spend = the only honest willingness-to-pay signal

questions.question: How often does the trigger event fire for one persona per year — once, monthly, daily?
Will this persona naturally refer peers? Why or why not?
questions.rationale: referral mechanics depend on whether the act of using is visible

questions.question: Three direct competitors — the ones a customer would naturally compare you to. Name them with URLs to their pricing pages.
questions.rationale: founders who haven't read competitor pricing have done no work

questions.question: Two indirect competitors — different category, same job done (e.g., Notion vs. Word + folder structure).
questions.rationale: indirect competition is the most-underestimated category

questions.question: Two substitutes — different solution shape, same outcome.
The "do nothing" competitor: how much pain does the customer currently absorb without buying anything? Why is that pain insufficient today?
questions.rationale: "do nothing" is the most common winner

questions.question: Pick the dimension you will win on: features, price, distribution, brand, integration, customer service, or speed-to-value. One — not two.
questions.rationale: the multi-dimensional pitch loses to the one-dimensional pitch

questions.question: For each direct competitor, name one thing they do *better* than you ever will. Honest answer.
questions.rationale: humility check, and forces founder to know the competitor

questions.question: For each direct competitor, name one thing you do better that the customer actually cares about.
If your largest competitor cut prices 50% on launch day, what changes about your plan?
If your largest competitor copies your differentiating feature in 90 days, what's your moat?
How easy is it for the customer to switch back to the competitor or to nothing? Low / med / high.
Who supplies your key inputs (API providers, content, talent)? What leverage do they have?
How hard is it for a new well-funded entrant to copy you in 18 months?
What is your unfair advantage — Lean Canvas block 9? Must be something that can't be easily copied or bought.
What's the most positive review of a competitor that scares you? Quote the line.
questions.rationale: scariest review = the strongest competitor signal

questions.question: What's the most negative review of a competitor that energizes you? Quote the line.
What's the most recently funded competitor's round size, year, and stated focus?
questions.rationale: funding levels predict feature pace and pricing posture

questions.question: Are competitors hiring aggressively (LinkedIn, AngelList)? What roles?
What did each direct competitor ship in the last 90 days?
Have any of your prospects switched away from a named competitor? What was the reason? Quote.
Which switching friction does your customer have to overcome to leave a competitor for you? Migration cost, lock-in, sunk cost, learned habit. Name it.
If Google / Apple / Microsoft / Amazon launched this tomorrow, what survives?
What's the smallest event in the customer's life that would push them from "doing nothing" to "buying something"?
questions.rationale: trigger = the moment the buy decision becomes possible

questions.question: Three direct competitors and how each positions itself in one sentence (from their homepage h1).
How crowded is the direct space — low / med / high? Defend with the count and the share.
Describe the problem in one sentence using the customer's own words — not yours.
questions.rationale: founders narrate in their own jargon by default

questions.question: On a 1-10 pain scale, where is this problem in the customer's life today? If less than 7, why will they pay?
What's the cost of the problem today — in dollars, hours, or stress — for a single customer per month?
Fill in: *"We help [customer] solve [problem] by [unique method] so they can [outcome]."* All four blanks.
"Compared to what" test: when you say "faster" or "cheaper" or "easier," compared to what specifically?
In 60 words, paint a customer's day before your product and after.
Write three tagline candidates, pick one. We're not committing — just forcing crispness.
questions.rationale: if three taglines are equally tepid, the UVP isn't sharp

questions.question: Explain this to a smart 12-year-old in one paragraph.
A stranger says "tell me about your company in 30 seconds" — go. (Timed.)
questions.rationale: if you can't, the homepage can't either

questions.question: What does the customer have to *believe* about the world for your UVP to land? Name the belief.
If that belief is wrong (or wrong for half your prospects), what's the fallback message?
Pick three competitor taglines and write one sentence on what each gets wrong.
questions.rationale: what competitors get wrong = your wedge

questions.question: Three of your prospects, in their own words, describe the problem differently. Quote each verbatim.
questions.rationale: variance in customer framing exposes hidden segments

questions.question: Will the same UVP work for both buyer and user, or do you need two?
How do you position relative to category leaders — replacement, complement, lower tier, premium tier, adjacent?
Draft a 6-10 word homepage headline.
questions.rationale: the headline is 90% of the conversion

questions.question: First 30 seconds of a customer demo — what do you show?
"I already tried X." What's your one-sentence reply?
questions.rationale: the most common objection is the one you must defeat first

questions.question: Same pitch with $200K hanging on the next 90 seconds — go. (Tightens further.)
questions.rationale: founder ROI on this exercise is high

questions.question: On a 1-10 urgency scale, when does the customer feel the pain — daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly?
In your UVP, the "unique method" must be specific. If you wrote "AI-powered" or "automated," replace it with the concrete mechanism.
Write one tagline you'd refuse to use because it sounds like everyone else.
List the 5-7 features that ship in MVP. No more than 7; if you have 8, cut one before answering.
For each MVP feature, write the one-sentence user-facing benefit. If you can't, it's not MVP.
For each MVP feature, name the single hypothesis it tests.
questions.rationale: MVP is a thesis-test, not a product

questions.question: List 10 features that *will* eventually be in the product but are *not* in MVP.
questions.rationale: the cut conversation must be explicit

questions.question: For each cut feature, name the cut rationale: "we can validate without it," "it doubles build time," "competitors don't have it so absence isn't fatal," or "we're not sure it's right yet."
Walk me through the happy-path user flow as a sequence of screens. Step by step. 5-12 steps.
What's the *one* thing the user must successfully do in MVP for the MVP to be considered shipped?
What's the MVP success criterion — the metric that says "ship it, you've proven enough"? Pick a number and a horizon (e.g., "100 weekly active users by week 6").
questions.rationale: success without a number is hope

questions.question: What's the MVP failure criterion — the metric that says "kill or pivot"? Pick a number and a horizon.
If you had to ship MVP in 4 weeks instead of 12, what comes out?
If you had 12 weeks instead of 4, what comes back in? (Forces the "MVP+ vs. MVP" boundary.)
questions.rationale: the boundary is where pressure lives

questions.question: Will MVP have a paywall, free tier, freemium, or just free?
Will MVP have an admin panel for you? A user-facing settings page? Both? Neither?
Any third-party integration in MVP that, if it breaks on launch day, blocks all users? Name it.
What's the manual fallback if a critical automation fails on day 1 — you doing it by hand?
questions.rationale: "do things that don't scale" is MVP wisdom

questions.question: What happens to MVP features that no one uses in week 4? Remove, ignore, double-down?
What data do you collect from day 1? Name three signals you instrument.
What data would you love to collect but cut from MVP for cost or privacy reasons?
What accessibility floor must MVP meet — WCAG AA, WCAG A, none?
Mobile-first, web-first, both?
How many languages does MVP support at launch — one, two, three?
On a 1-10 polish scale, what does MVP ship at? Where 10 = Apple, 5 = "looks like a startup," 1 = "looks like a hackathon."
Is there a "demo-friendly" feature that exists only because it makes the screen-share look good? Name it; defend or cut.
questions.rationale: demo theater is real; expose it

questions.question: The *first* feature you add post-MVP, assuming MVP works. Just one.
questions.rationale: post-MVP discipline must start before MVP ships

questions.question: By month 12, the product looks like *what* — list the 5-10 features that exist at end-of-year-1 but not in MVP.
Which of those 1-year features are gated on MVP signal (we only build if MVP proves X)?
Which are unconditional — we build regardless of MVP signal? Why?
questions.rationale: unconditional should be the smaller set

questions.question: By year 5, what does the company look like — revenue range, headcount range, customer count, market position?
questions.rationale: ambition must be vivid to be testable

questions.question: By year 5, what adjacent products live alongside the core? Name 1-3.
By year 5: product company, platform, marketplace, or services-led firm? Pick one.
questions.rationale: the four shapes have different fundraising and exit paths

questions.question: If MVP fails the success criterion, what's pivot option A? Option B?
The *first* thing you build post-MVP, assuming MVP works. Pick exactly one.
The *worst* thing you could do in year 1 — the move that would feel right but kill compounding.
Will you raise capital in year 1? If yes, what amount, against what milestone?
questions.rationale: capital plan affects every roadmap decision

questions.question: Year-5 vision: one paragraph, as if a journalist is profiling the company.
What MVP architectural decisions would you regret most by year 3 if you don't address them?
When does international expansion start — year 1, year 2, year 3? Name the first country.
When does the enterprise tier launch — never, year 1, year 2?
When does the second product launch — year 1, year 2, year 5?
questions.rationale: "do one thing well" vs. multi-product company

questions.question: Year 1 hiring plan vs. year 2 hiring plan — two roles each.
When do you form a board — never, year 1, year 2 (post-Series A)?
What's the one line you'd want the company to be famous for in 5 years?
Beyond MVP success/failure, what leading indicator at month 4 would force a pivot conversation?
questions.rationale: pivots happen on leading signals, not lagging

questions.question: Pick one 1-year feature you're tempted to put in MVP. Defend keeping it in 1-year.
questions.rationale: the fence is enforced by the founder, with the consultant's help

questions.question: Preferred languages and frameworks for frontend and backend. Or: "let the engineering team pick" — also a complete answer.
Preferred database: relational (Postgres/MySQL), document (Mongo/Dynamo), graph, time-series, search-first (Elastic/Algolia), or hybrid. Defend in one sentence.
questions.rationale: wrong DB shape = years of refactor

questions.question: List all third-party services you assume in MVP: auth, payments, email, SMS, analytics, observability, CDN, CMS.
For each, name the specific provider (Stripe vs. Lemonsqueezy vs. Paddle, Auth0 vs. Clerk vs. self-built).
Are you exposing an API to external developers in MVP? Year 1? Year 5?
Mobile-first, web-first, both? Native (iOS/Android), React Native, web-only-PWA — pick one for MVP.
Build vs. buy on each major capability — name three you'd buy and three you must build. Defend each.
Any "no-go" technologies because of the customer's stack? (Some buyers refuse PHP, some refuse open-source DBs, some require on-prem.)
Single monolithic deploy, or already 2-3 services at MVP?
questions.rationale: premature microservices is the most common architecture mistake

questions.question: AWS, GCP, Azure, multi-cloud, or single-tenant on-prem.
Customer hosts (on-prem / single-tenant cloud) or you host (multi-tenant SaaS)?
Latency budget for the primary user action — p50 and p95 in ms.
Acceptable downtime per quarter — minutes? hours?
Any AI/LLM in the product? Which model, which provider, what's the fallback when the provider rate-limits?
Data residency requirements — must stay in EU, US, India?
Encryption-at-rest expectations — default cloud encryption, customer-managed keys, HSM?
Secret management — AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Doppler, environment variables only?
CI/CD ambition — manual deploys, GitHub Actions, full GitOps with environments?
Observability stack — Datadog, New Relic, Grafana/Prometheus/Loki, Sentry, in-house?
questions.rationale: without obs you can't run an MVP in prod

questions.question: Feature-flag system — LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith, Statsig, in-house, none?
What's the "refactor first when we have time" item — the technical debt you'd pay down first if a free week appeared?
Internal API style — REST, GraphQL, tRPC, gRPC, mixed?
Event-driven or request/response? If event-driven, what's the bus — Kafka, Kinesis, SQS, in-process?
questions.rationale: event-driven is overkill for most MVPs

questions.question: How do you handle vendor breakage — when a critical third-party API changes contracts unexpectedly?
Day-1 user count expectation. Real number, not aspirational.
Month-3 user count expectation.
Month-12 user count expectation.
Peak concurrent users at scale — the highest realistic instant.
questions.rationale: concurrency != total users; sizing follows peaks

questions.question: Largest single data write per user action — bytes or rows?
Total data volume after 12 months — GB or TB?
Time-to-first-byte budget for cold-cache requests.
Time-to-interactive budget for the primary page on a mid-tier mobile over 4G.
Acceptable error rate (4xx/5xx) per 1000 requests.
Backup / restore RPO and RTO.
When does the product need to be 99.9% vs. 99.95% vs. 99.99%? Pick a year.
Pick a scale trigger that initiates a re-architecture conversation — e.g., "when we cross 10,000 concurrent users."
Peak-to-average traffic ratio — daily, weekly, seasonal?
Batch-job latency — overnight is fine, hourly, minutes?
If search is core, search-index size target and query latency.
Image/asset CDN strategy — Cloudflare, Fastly, CloudFront, none?
Multi-region failover scope — same region only, multi-region active-passive, active-active?
Pre-launch load-test plan — number of synthetic users, scenario count, pass/fail criteria.
questions.rationale: load tests catch what staging doesn't

questions.question: Three adjectives for brand voice. Plus one adjective the brand must absolutely not be ("not corporate," "not cute," "not preachy").
questions.rationale: positive + negative = sharper than positive alone

questions.question: Three reference brands (any industry) whose voice you'd happily steal. Paste URLs.
Visual style poles — pick where you sit on each axis: editorial ↔ dense, playful ↔ serious, warm ↔ cool, motion-rich ↔ motion-free.
Color disposition — monochrome, warm-palette, cool-palette, high-saturation, brand-color-led.
questions.rationale: color is the fastest brand recall lever

questions.question: Typography preference — serif, sans, hybrid, mono. Reference (e.g., "Inter, like Linear").
Logo: do you have one? Do you need one for MVP? Or text-mark only?
questions.rationale: MVPs ship without logos all the time

questions.question: Voice in error messages — apologetic, neutral-functional, witty, transparent-technical.
Three brands whose tone you'd hate to be confused with.
Will the brand evolve in year 2 — softer, more enterprise, more consumer? Or hold?
Brand name: locked, working-title, or unresolved? If unresolved, set a decide-by date.
Domain owned? Trademark filed? (Cross-ref B.14.)
Social handles secured on the channels that matter?
Will the brand support dark mode at launch?
Mascot, illustration style, or photography?
Imagery — custom photography, stock, illustration, none?
Iconography source — custom, Lucide, Heroicons, Phosphor, none?
Three microcopy laws you commit to (e.g., "never use 'sorry' twice in one screen").
Email-template tone — same as in-product, more formal, less?
questions.rationale: email is the brand's longest medium

questions.question: Social-post template tone and visual style.
Where should the brand sit by year 3 — same coordinates, premiumized, broader?
Brand name: easy to pronounce in one second on the phone? Spell-friendly in non-English contexts?
questions.rationale: name friction is invisible until it costs you

questions.question: Design system source — build from scratch, fork Tailwind UI, shadcn/ui, Linear-style custom, Untitled UI, other?
Six article/page titles that exist on the site at launch — don't write the articles, just titles.
Content cadence after launch — daily, weekly, monthly, "whenever I feel like it." Be honest.
questions.rationale: capacity planning depends on the honest answer

questions.question: Who writes content — founder, in-house writer, contractor, AI-assisted-then-edited, or "leave CMS empty for now."
Three Google queries you'd want to rank top-3 for within 12 months.
questions.rationale: if you can't name them, SEO isn't a plan

questions.question: Geographies that actually matter for SEO — global, single country, single city.
Will you run paid ads at launch? Channel and monthly budget if yes.
If paid, what's your CPL or CPA target before you scale?
Email list — start collecting from day 1, day 30, or never.
questions.rationale: email is the most durable channel; start early

questions.question: Newsletter cadence and audience size goal at month 12.
Referral mechanic at launch — affiliate, in-product invite, share-card, none.
questions.rationale: referral is the cheapest channel that exists

questions.question: Community presence — Discord, Slack, subreddit, forum, none.
PR plan at launch — Hacker News, Product Hunt, podcast circuit, embargoed press, nothing.
Will you publish a public roadmap (Linear public board, Productboard, your own)?
"Founder content" strategy — Twitter/X, LinkedIn, blog, podcast appearances. Pick two.
Backlink strategy at month 6 — guest posts, sponsorships, organic, none.
Schema-markup priority — yes/no, which types (Product, FAQ, HowTo, Article)?
Open Graph asset set — single, per-template, per-article?
Sitemap strategy — automatic, hand-curated, none?
Content-refresh cadence — quarterly, annually, never?
Attribution model — last-touch, multi-touch, manual lift studies, none?
What's the one signal you'd watch in month 1 that says "SEO is working"?
questions.rationale: SEO has a 6-12 month delay; leading indicators matter

questions.question: Target k-factor (invites per existing user that convert) — 0.1, 0.3, 0.7?
questions.rationale: virality is binary above k=1, decay below

questions.question: Which paid channel do you test first if organic underperforms — Google Search, Meta, LinkedIn, TikTok, Reddit?
questions.rationale: sequence matters; one at a time

questions.question: Pick one writer / publication whose voice your content tries to echo. Name them.
Starting capital available to build MVP — bootstrapped, personal savings, friends-and-family, pre-seed, seed. Amount.
Founder salary in year 1 — taking nothing, taking a stipend, taking market rate.
Total MVP build budget including any contracted help.
Monthly burn rate post-MVP — explicit number.
Runway months at current burn.
questions.rationale: the single number that says how long you have

questions.question: Capital strategy — bootstrap to profitability, raise pre-seed, raise seed, raise Series A within 18 months. Pick one.
questions.rationale: the strategy determines every hire and every spend

questions.question: If raising, target round size and target valuation cap.
Will you use the YC standard post-money SAFE for seed?
questions.rationale: the post-money SAFE has guaranteed dilution; founders often miss this

questions.question: Aware that one $1M post-money SAFE at $10M cap = 10% guaranteed dilution before Series A? Confirm.
CAC target (year 1).
LTV estimate with assumptions (gross margin, retention, ARPU).
LTV:CAC ratio target — should exceed 3:1 for healthy SaaS.
CAC payback period target — months. Ideally under 18 for SMB SaaS.
Gross margin target at scale.
Revenue year 1. Defend this hardest.
questions.rationale: year 1 = the only one you'll be held to

questions.question: Revenue year 2 and year 3.
Bottom-up logic for year 1 revenue — customers × ARPU × months? Show the multiplication.
questions.rationale: every defended revenue forecast is bottom-up

questions.question: Largest single line item of monthly spend post-launch.
questions.rationale: the line you'd cut if forced

questions.question: When do you become cash-flow positive — month X?
questions.rationale: profitability date is a forcing function

questions.question: Founder dilution tolerance — how much equity are you willing to give up to Series A?
Round size > $3M triggers board-seat negotiations — confirm awareness.
409A / fair-value triggers — when does the first 409A happen?
Option pool sizing at seed — 10%, 12.5%, 15%?
questions.rationale: the pool comes out of founder equity in most rounds

questions.question: Advisor equity allocation — total %, per-advisor %, vesting schedule.
Write the "if we never raise" version of the plan in one paragraph.
Gross revenue retention target at month 12.
questions.rationale: GRR predicts NRR predicts valuation multiple

questions.question: Founding team count today. Solo, 2-person, 3+?
questions.rationale: YC notes solo founders need stronger traction; team size affects every diligence

questions.question: Founder roles — CEO, CTO, CPO, head-of-business. Who does what, today.
Cofounder gap — what skillset is missing that matters most?
First 3 hires after MVP launches — role, timing trigger, target compensation.
Support model in MVP — founder answers email, shared inbox, ticketing tool, none.
Support SLA — response time, resolution time.
Weekly all-hands, monthly metrics review, quarterly planning — yes/no for each.
Contractors — design, content, legal, ops? Name the role and the budget.
Remote-only, hybrid, co-located the team in year 1?
Time-zone constraints for hires?
Performance-review cadence year 1.
Founder-CEO succession plan — none yet (most common), defined trigger, defined timeline.
questions.rationale: even the founder's exit is a decision to defer or define

questions.question: Equity vesting schedule for founders and early team — standard 4y/1y cliff, longer, custom?
Advisor roster — names, areas of help, equity allocated.
Fractional execs — fractional CFO, CMO, COO. Yes/no, when, budget?
questions.rationale: fractional talent fills strategic gaps cheaply

questions.question: Intern strategy — yes/no, summer / part-time / never.
What roles would you outsource that you won't, and why?
For each of (build, sell, support, ops, finance, legal), name the directly-responsible founder.
Async-first or sync-first culture?
Founder personal-financial runway (separate from company runway). How long can each founder personally afford to do this?
Legal entity formed? Type (Delaware C-Corp, LLC, foreign jurisdiction)? If not yet, by when.
Jurisdictions where customers will be served at launch — country list.
Jurisdictions where customer data physically lives.
questions.rationale: data residency = both legal and architectural

questions.question: GDPR-in-scope (any EU customers or data)?
questions.rationale: if yes, the compliance surface is non-trivial

questions.question: CCPA-in-scope (California customers above the threshold)?
HIPAA-in-scope (any PHI touched)? If yes, this is a much larger compliance surface — confirm.
PCI-DSS-in-scope (storing card data ourselves vs. tokenizing via Stripe)?
SOC 2 — required at launch, year 1, year 2, never?
Age-gating required? COPPA-in-scope (US under-13)?
Trademark — name + logo, filed in which jurisdictions, status?
Domain ownership confirmed (no defensive variants needed)?
Patent strategy — file, don't file, defensive publication only.
questions.rationale: patent strategy is binary; default is "don't" for SaaS

questions.question: ToS and Privacy Policy — drafted by whom (lawyer, template, AI-drafted-then-reviewed)?
Contractor agreements — IP assignment language present?
questions.rationale: the most common founder mistake is unwritten IP assignment

questions.question: Open-source license obligations — anything copyleft (GPL/AGPL) in the stack we need to be careful about?
Liability insurance — E&O, cyber, general — at launch or post-launch?
questions.rationale: uninsured launches happen but are bets

questions.question: Export-control issues if international — encryption export classification?
Industry-specific licensing (financial services, healthcare, legal services, gambling) — apply, partner, avoid.
questions.rationale: licensed industries take 18+ months; default is partner

questions.question: Cookie-banner posture — strict-consent, soft-consent, none?
DPA template for B2B customers — yours or theirs?
Sub-processor disclosures — public list maintained?
Breach-notification plan — within 72h (GDPR), 30 days (CCPA), other?
questions.rationale: the plan exists or it doesn't

questions.question: NDA template for partners — yours or theirs?
What litigation would you most fear, and what's the pre-emptive mitigation?
Cap table managed in — Carta, Pulley, manual spreadsheet?
Do you need a foreign subsidiary (e.g., India entity, UK Ltd) in year 1?
questions.rationale: multi-entity structures cost real money and time

questions.question: Imagine 18 months from now — the venture has clearly failed. Why did it fail? List the top 5 reasons in past tense.
For each premortem reason, what would you have had to do differently in months 1-3 to prevent it?
Top 5 risks ranked by impact × likelihood. Cite each.
questions.rationale: ranked risk register beats unranked list

questions.question: For each risk, name a mitigation — concrete action, not aspiration.
Single biggest "external" risk you have no control over — funding climate, regulatory, platform-dependency, talent market.
Single biggest "internal" risk you do control — cofounder conflict, technical debt, focus-drift, hiring.
Kill criterion in writing: "we kill or pivot if, by month X, we have not achieved Y."
Pivot-vs-persevere trigger — name the leading indicator that triggers a serious pivot conversation.
The "one customer / one channel" concentration risk — name yours.

If your personal financial runway runs out 3 months before the company hits its kill/pivot date, what is the contingency — second job, bridge loan from family, accept the kill earlier, walk into a worse fundraise? Name the action and the trigger month.
questions.rationale: founder-personal-finance shock is the most common silent killer (cross-ref B13-Q20 which captures the raw runway months)

questions.question: Founder burnout risk — what's your recovery protocol?
Reputational risk if MVP launches and is terrible — soft launch, hard launch, stealth-then-launch.
questions.rationale: launch posture is a risk-management decision

questions.question: Single point of technical failure (a vendor, an API, a person) — name it, plan a mitigation.
questions.rationale: SPOF = the first risk a CTO finds

questions.question: PR-incident plan — who responds, in what time, on what channel?
questions.rationale: PR crises happen in hours, not days

questions.question: Security-incident plan — detection → containment → notification → post-mortem.
questions.rationale: breach response is a measurable maturity gate

questions.question: Churn-spike trigger — what's the weekly-churn threshold that forces an emergency conversation?
Founder-leave scenario — if your cofounder leaves in month 4, what changes?
questions.rationale: cofounder-leave is the most common silent-killer

questions.question: What would Marc Andreessen tell you to do differently? (External-perspective test.)
questions.rationale: stepping out of your own POV

questions.question: If your primary acquisition channel fails entirely by month 4, what's the fallback playbook?
If you pivot, in which of three directions — narrower ICP, adjacent persona, different problem same persona?
questions.rationale: pivots happen along axes; pre-naming them speeds decisions

questions.question: North-star metric — the single number that, if it grows, means everything else is working. Pick one.
questions.rationale: companies without a north star measure everything and improve nothing

questions.question: Why that number and not a different one?
Three leading indicators that precede the north-star number — daily/weekly observable.
questions.rationale: lagging metrics tell you too late

questions.question: PMF measurement methodology — Sean Ellis 40% rule, retention curve, NPS, gross-revenue retention, none.
questions.rationale: Jackson / Reforge — measure PMF, don't guess

questions.question: Metric you *won't* obsess over even though competitors do.
Reporting cadence — daily, weekly, monthly.
Threshold for "great" vs. "good" vs. "bad" on the north star, week 6 post-launch.
Same thresholds, month 6 and month 12.
Disconfirming evidence: what would you have to see in usage data that would force you to re-examine the entire thesis?
Vanity-metric trap: which metric is most tempting to brag about that doesn't actually predict success?
questions.rationale: name the lie you're tempted to tell

questions.question: Cohort-retention curve shape you'd be happy to see at week 6, week 12, week 24.
The "wow moment" — the single action a new user takes that, statistically, predicts they stay. Name it.
questions.rationale: the wow moment is the conversion target

questions.question: Dashboard ownership — founder, marketing, an analytics hire, none?
Alert-threshold setup — at what number do you wake up at 2am?
If you had to pick exactly one metric to ship publicly to investors, what would it be?
Sean Ellis "how disappointed would you be if this product disappeared" — % "very disappointed" target, week-12 and month-6.
